model_only.commentary: J.P. Morgan favors higher-quality credit because spreads remain tight versus history, making improved credit quality the attractive expression. Evidence: The group also saw an opportunity to go up in credit quality at a time when spreads generally remain narrow compared with history, especially across the European banking capital structure.. Locator: p.3 (Global Fixed Income Views 2Q 2026).

J.P. Morgan sees the oil shock as temporary, with Brent elevated in Q2 but normalizing over Q3-Q4, so the two-sided path nets to neutral for oil. Evidence: In our own base case scenario, in which Brent crude averages around $100 in Q2 before retreating to more normal levels over the course of Q3 and Q4, the global economy would likely be able to move past the energy shock and deliver solid growth.. Locator: p.1 (Global Asset Allocation Views 2Q 2026). Checker: unconfirmed (Quote is a macro base-case oil price path (elevated then retreating); the net direction reads as mildly downward as much as neutral, so N is plausible but not decisive.).

J.P. Morgan maintains a risk-on allocation stance and explicitly includes an equity overweight over the 12-18 month horizon. Evidence: This view sets the foundation for our continued risk-on stance, including an equity overweight, over a 12-18 month investment horizon.. Locator: p.1 (Global Asset Allocation Views 2Q 2026).

J.P. Morgan favors shorter duration in fixed income as it expects yield curves to steepen. Evidence: Our equity outlook remains moderately pro-risk, reflecting our base case of solid economic growth and structural tailwinds from global fiscal stimulus and AI adoption. In fixed income, we are leaning into shorter duration as yield curves steepen. Credit positioning is neutral, reflecting the asymmetric distribution of returns in the current environment (Exhibit 2).. Locator: p.3 (Global Asset Allocation Views 2Q 2026).

In fixed income, we are leaning into shorter duration as yield curves steepen. Credit positioning is neutral, reflecting the asymmetric distribution of returns in the current environment (Exhibit 2).. Locator: p.3 (Global Asset Allocation Views 2Q 2026).

J.P. Morgan maintains a neutral high yield stance because downside return asymmetry offsets attractive carry. Evidence: With asymmetric returns to the downside, we maintain a neutral stance on high yield and emerging market debt (Exhibit 3).. Locator: p.3 (Global Asset Allocation Views 2Q 2026).

gt_only.commentary: The GFICC team identifies front-end government bonds as the most compelling opportunity in fixed income. Short-dated curves have repriced 60-100 bps higher on hawkish central bank rhetoric, creating attractive entry points. Front-end positioning serves a dual purpose: income generation through elevated carry and a natural hedge against a broader derisking event. The team explicitly prefers the front end over the long end.

With the fed funds rate at 3.625% and the Fed explicitly on hold until more information about the conflict's impact emerges, the front end of the US Treasury curve offers better risk-reward than the long end. Chair Powell has indicated the vast majority of the FOMC do not see a hike as the base case, anchoring short-dated yields. The 10-year is expected to range 3.75%-4.25%, limiting upside further out the curve.

Agency mortgage-backed securities offer yield with the backing of government-sponsored enterprises, providing a credit quality advantage during periods of elevated uncertainty. The team highlights agency MBS as part of their broader securitized credit overweight, noting that structural protections and US consumer resilience make this a preferred source of carry.

The team sees an opportunity to upgrade credit quality across portfolios as spreads remain narrow compared to history. Higher-quality investment grade issuers offer better risk-adjusted carry in a more uncertain macro backdrop. This is particularly relevant across the European banking capital structure, where the team recommends moving up from subordinated to senior exposure.

Cash and money market instruments offer attractive yields at current policy rates but face reinvestment risk as the team expects eventual rate cuts once the oil shock feeds through to weaker demand. The team does not advocate reducing risk entirely to cash, preferring to remain active in markets.

The team's call to "go up in credit quality, especially across the European banking capital structure" is a direct signal to reduce subordinated bank debt exposure. With recession probability rising to 25% and the ECB potentially hiking into a slowdown, lower tiers of the banking capital stack face disproportionate risk.

Narrow spreads relative to history offer insufficient compensation for the rising probability of recession in the UK and Europe. If the ECB and BoE hike rather than cut, the damage to European corporates — particularly highly leveraged issuers — could be severe. The team's bias to upgrade credit quality further argues against lower-rated credit.
